synthesis.vibe: Like watching your coolest friend cook dinner — effortless, unfussy, and just satisfying enough to make you hungry and slightly jealous.
synthesis.content_type: Home cooking recipe reel (single dish, start to finish)
synthesis.creator_archetype: The relatable home chef — confident but not performative, cooking for himself and theoretically for someone else
synthesis.hook_formula: Creator stands at the kitchen counter facing camera, ingredient(s) already on the board in front of him, no speaking or big gesture — just a deadpan or focused look directly into lens. A short, lowercase white text overlay appears on his chest/torso area within the first 0.5s that reframes the recipe with a personal or self-deprecating angle (e.g. 'my future wife', 'Don't hate me', 'deleted'). Energy is calm-confident, not hype. The hook is the TEXT doing the emotional work while the visual stays still and grounded.

cooking_narrative.description: A tight, linear recipe walkthrough that follows the cooking process chronologically but strips out all dead time. The creator stays in frame for most shots rather than doing pure ingredient close-ups — you always know he's the one making it. Ends with a hero plating shot and a first-bite reaction.
sequence: Ingredient reveal — creator holds or presents the hero ingredient at the counter, text overlay sets the premise/joke
Prep close-up — cutting, patting dry, seasoning; fast cuts showing hand actions
Heat/sear/cook — stove or oven action, often a low-angle or eye-level shot of the pan with creator behind it
Mid-cook seasoning or technique moment — shaking spices dramatically, flipping, stirring
Plating sequence — creator arranges food on a white plate, deliberate and careful hand movements
Final dish reveal — overhead or eye-level static shot of the completed plate
First bite / tasting reaction — creator eats on camera, minimal expression, let the face do the talking
cooking_narrative.what_they_skip: Grocery shopping, full ingredient lists on screen, precise measurements, boiling water waiting shots, oven pre-heating, washing produce, cleaning up, any verbal explanation of technique. No voiceover narration — the cooking IS the content.
cooking_narrative.money_shot: The final plated dish shot just before or during the first bite — always a clean white plate with the food styled (not just dumped). Appears at the 85% mark. The hero shot is eye-level or slight low-angle with creator leaning in to garnish or taste, giving scale and human context to the food.
cooking_narrative.pacing_within_steps: He lingers slightly on the sear/cook step — the pan action gets 1.5-2.5s cuts to let the sizzle and color register. The plating step also gets a beat of breathing room (1.2-1.5s cuts). Everything else is rapid-fire 0.5-0.8s cuts. He does NOT linger on raw prep steps.

visual_identity.shot_composition: Predominantly medium shots (waist-to-head) of the creator with the food in the lower third of frame — creator is always present as a physical anchor. Interspersed with tight action close-ups of hands/food at counter level. Ratio is roughly 60% creator-in-frame medium shots, 30% close-up action shots, 10% beauty/static food shots. No overhead drone-style cooking shots. Camera is usually positioned at counter height or slightly below, looking up at creator — makes him feel larger and more authoritative without being try-hard.
visual_identity.camera_work: Mix of handheld (slightly loose, natural micro-shake) for cooking action shots and static/tripod for the talking-head counter moments. No gimbal-smooth gliding. The handheld feel is intentional — keeps it grounded and personal. No whip pans or zoom cuts.
visual_identity.lighting_style: Warm kitchen ambient — recessed ceiling lights doing the heavy lifting, no visible ring light or softbox. Golden-warm color temperature. Slightly underexposed (brightness consistently 0.43-0.48) which gives a cozy, evening-at-home feel rather than bright studio. Shadows are soft, not dramatic.
visual_identity.transition_style: Almost exclusively hard cuts — no dissolves, no whip pans, no transitions. The rhythm IS the editing. Occasional match-action cut where movement in one shot carries into the next (e.g. hand reaching down cuts to hand already at the pan). No motion graphics or stylized transitions whatsoever.

pacing_pattern.description: Fast baseline with deliberate breathing room. Opens with 1-2 slightly slower cuts to establish the premise, then drops into rapid 0.5-0.8s cuts for prep and action. Mid-reel has occasional 1.5-2.6s 'rest' cuts on meaningful moments (sizzle, season, stir). Closes with slightly slower pacing on the money shot and reaction.
pacing_pattern.opening_cuts: 2-3 cuts in first 3 seconds — first cut is ~0.5-0.6s (hook frame), second is ~1.0-1.1s (establish), third drops fast. Not frenetic from frame one — gives the hook text time to land.
pacing_pattern.body_rhythm: Rapid-fire 0.6-1.0s cuts with occasional 1.5-2.5s anchor cuts on key cooking moments. Pacing is action-driven, not beat-synced. Cuts happen when the action changes, not strictly on the beat. The overall feel is rhythmic but organic rather than mechanical.
pacing_pattern.closing_style: Slows slightly for the plating and final dish reveal. Last 10-15 seconds tend to have longer cuts (1.0-1.5s) to let the food and reaction breathe. Ends on creator eating or the finished plate — no title card, no CTA graphic, no music fade-out moment.

color_recipe.description: Warm kitchen glow — golden tones pushed in the reds/yellows, slightly crushed blacks, mild saturation boost that makes food look appetizing without looking oversaturated. Feels like late afternoon kitchen light even if it is evening. Not moody, not clinical — just warm and lived-in.

text_recipe.description: Text overlays are short, lowercase (or minimal caps), white, no background box, no drop shadow — just clean type sitting in the lower-third chest/torso zone of the creator. Text does NOT describe what is happening visually — it provides the emotional subtext, the joke, the internal monologue, or the recipe context that the creator never says out loud. Examples: 'my future wife', 'on vacation', 'plate look', 'final dish', 'to the oven', 'tonight'. Text is sparse per cut — one phrase at a time, not a running caption. Creator never speaks these words; the text is a parallel narrative layer.

structure_template.description: A tight 60-78 second single-dish cooking reel with a text-hook premise, rapid-cut process, and a payoff eating moment. No fluff, no intro, no outro card.
structure_template.hook_style: Creator at counter, hero ingredient in frame, deadpan expression or focused eyes-down, lowercase text overlay on chest that reframes the dish with a personal/humorous angle. Static or very slight handheld. One hard cut within the first 1 second.
structure_template.body_structure: Sequential cooking steps in real process order — prep, cook, season, cook more, plate. Each step gets 2-5 rapid cuts. No step gets more than 8-10 seconds. The pan/heat moment is the visual climax of the cooking section (bubbling, sizzling, caramelizing) and gets the most screen time in the body. Creator is in frame for at least 50% of body shots.
structure_template.outro_style: Plating close-up followed by creator eating on camera. Tasting reaction is understated — slight nod, maybe a squint of satisfaction. No verbal wrap-up, no 'recipe in bio', no explicit CTA. The last frame is the creator eating or the finished plate. Music continues under the cut to black.

signature_moves: The Chest Text Hook — lowercase white text planted on creator's chest/torso in the first 0.5s that delivers the emotional premise of the video without any spoken words
Deadpan Counter Stand — creator opens every reel standing still at the counter with ingredient already present, no setup theatrics, no pointing at camera, just exists in the space
Action-Driven Hard Cuts — every single cut is motivated by a change in physical action (hand movement, pan flip, new ingredient), never cutting on stillness
Creator-Anchored Framing — even close-up shots often include part of the creator's body (forearms, torso) so the human presence never fully disappears from frame
The Deliberate Slow Cut Before the Payoff — intentionally holds 1.5-2.5s on the sizzling pan or the plating moment right before the final reveal, creating a micro-tension release
Parallel Text Narration — text overlays run as a separate emotional/comedic track that never literally describes the visual, functioning like internal monologue subtitles
avoid: Voiceover or talking-head explanation — he never narrates the recipe verbally; adding a voiceover would completely break the silent-confident aesthetic
Motion graphics, animated transitions, or text with backgrounds/outlines — all text is clean plain white lowercase; any styled text box or whip-pan transition would kill the casual authenticity

Overhead-only or food-photography-style shots without the creator present — his physical presence is the brand; a reel that becomes a pure food B-roll video loses the 'cooking for someone I care about' persona entirely
replication_instructions: HOOK (0-3s): Open on a static or very slightly handheld shot of the creator standing at the kitchen counter, waist-to-head framing. The hero ingredient should already be on the board in front of them. Do not add any intro animation. Within the first 0.5s, place a lowercase white text overlay (no background, no shadow, font weight regular to medium) on the creator's chest/torso area. The text should NOT describe the food — it should deliver a personal angle, joke, or context that makes the viewer feel like they're in on something (e.g. 'cooking for no one', 'don't judge me', 'she asked for this'). Hold this shot for ~1.0-1.1s then hard cut.

APPLY COLOR GRADE FIRST before editing cuts: Set brightness to -0.030, contrast to 1.318, saturation to 0.738, red gain to 1.084, blue gain to 1.0. This creates the warm kitchen-glow look. Apply this as a global LUT or adjustment layer across the entire timeline before making any cuts.

STRUCTURE THE TIMELINE in this order: ingredient reveal (3-6s) → prep/cutting (8-12s) → seasoning (4-6s) → heat/cook (10-15s) → mid-cook action like stirring or flipping (6-8s) → plating (8-10s) → final dish reveal (3-4s) → first bite reaction (4-6s). Total target: 60-75 seconds.

CUTTING RHYTHM: Default cut length is 0.6-1.0s for all action shots. Deliberately slow down to 1.5-2.5s ONLY on: (a) the pan sizzling at peak heat, (b) the plating arrangement moment, (c) the first bite reaction. Everything else should feel rapid. If a shot feels too long at 1 second, cut it. Use action as your cut trigger — cut the moment a hand movement completes or a new action begins.

SHOT SELECTION: Prioritize shots where the creator's hands AND face or torso are visible simultaneously. For close-up shots of food/pans, make sure at least one forearm or hand is in frame. Avoid pure food-only beauty shots except for the final dish reveal. Shoot from counter height or slightly below — never directly overhead.

TEXT OVERLAYS: Place 4-8 text overlays throughout the reel. Keep them lowercase, white, no styling. Position every overlay in the lower-third of the frame (roughly where the creator's chest sits in medium shots). Each text phrase should be 1-4 words maximum. The text should narrate the subtext of what's happening, not describe it literally. Examples for cooking moments: during seasoning write 'no measuring', during plating write 'good enough', during eating write 'worth it'. Text timing: appear on the cut, disappear on the next cut.

MUSIC SELECTION: Choose mid-tempo tracks at 89-108 BPM with a relaxed groove or steady rhythm (not drop-heavy EDM, not acoustic folk). The music should feel like something playing in an apartment kitchen — present but not the main event. Do NOT cut strictly to the beat. Let action drive cuts; if cuts land on beats, treat it as a bonus not a requirement.

OUTRO: The final 10-15 seconds should slow down. Cut to creator plating or garnishing (1.5s cut), then the finished dish on the plate from eye level (2-3s), then the creator taking the first bite on camera (3-4s). Hold the eating reaction for slightly longer than feels comfortable — the underreaction IS the reaction. End hard cut on the eating moment. No fade out, no title card, no CTA screen.

WHAT TO CUT: Remove all shots of: waiting (water boiling, oven preheating), ingredient labels being read, measuring spoons or cups being used, any moment where the creator looks at the camera waiting for a cue, any shot longer than 3 seconds unless it is the eating reaction. When in doubt, cut it out — this style rewards omission.

edit_recipe.opening_style: 2-3 cuts in first 3 seconds — first cut is ~0.5-0.6s (hook frame), second is ~1.0-1.1s (establish), third drops fast. Not frenetic from frame one — gives the hook text time to land.
edit_recipe.closing_style: Slows slightly for the plating and final dish reveal. Last 10-15 seconds tend to have longer cuts (1.0-1.5s) to let the food and reaction breathe. Ends on creator eating or the finished plate — no title card, no CTA graphic, no music fade-out moment.
edit_recipe.hook_style: Creator at counter, hero ingredient in frame, deadpan expression or focused eyes-down, lowercase text overlay on chest that reframes the dish with a personal/humorous angle. Static or very slight handheld. One hard cut within the first 1 second.
